positioning: Manual-input, formula-based field test protocol library for Track.Lab. No AI coach, no automated prescription, no database dependency, no fake metrics, no medical diagnosis.

protocols.name: Cooper 12-Minute Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate aerobic fitness from the maximum distance covered in exactly 12 minutes, then optionally convert the result into estimated VO2max and pace benchmarks.
bestUsedFor: Intermediate runners who can run continuously for at least 20-30 minutes.
Users needing a simple field-based aerobic benchmark without lab equipment.
Track/flat-loop testing where distance can be measured reliably.
notIdealFor: New runners without a running base.
Users with recent illness, injury, chest symptoms, heat stress, or no safe measured route.
Users expecting lab-grade VO2max accuracy.

requiredInputs.label: Body weight for optional energy estimate

protocolSteps: Choose a flat 400 m track or reliably measured flat loop.
Avoid testing in extreme heat, poor air quality, strong wind, or heavy fatigue.
Start the timer for exactly 12:00.
Run the greatest sustainable distance possible without sprinting recklessly in the opening minutes.
Record total distance at 12:00 exactly.
Record optional HR, RPE, weather, and surface to compare future retests fairly.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 10-20 min easy jog + mobility drills + 3-4 relaxed 15-20 sec strides + 3-5 min easy reset before start.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run as far as possible in 12 minutes.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Start controlled for the first 2-3 minutes, settle hard, then increase effort late if sustainable.
validityChecks: duration must equal 720 seconds
distance must be measured, not guessed
route should be flat and repeatable
do not mix treadmill and outdoor results without a note
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest every 6-8 weeks under similar conditions if user chooses.

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
protocols.limitation: A 12-minute field estimate is not lab-measured VO2max. It is affected by pacing, motivation, weather, surface, GPS/track accuracy, and running economy.

protocols.name: 1-Mile Time Trial
protocols.purpose: Establish a short-distance benchmark for current speed, training pace calibration, and race-prediction formulas.
bestUsedFor: Runners who need a compact benchmark for mile/1500 m/5K pace modeling.
Users who have access to a track or accurately measured 1-mile route.
Beginners only if they already run continuously and can pace safely.
notIdealFor: Users with no running base.
Users using the result as a medical fitness test.
Testing in traffic, high heat, or poor surface conditions.
protocolSteps: Use a track if possible: 1609.344 m or accepted local mile route.
Warm up thoroughly because the test is short and intense.
Run hard but controlled; avoid going all-out in the first 400 m.
Record finish time and optional lap splits.
Use the result only as a manual input for formulas; do not auto-prescribe training.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 15-25 min easy jog + dynamic drills + 4 x 15-20 sec strides + 3-5 min easy reset.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run 1 mile as fast as safely possible with even or slight negative pacing.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: First 400 m controlled, middle 800 m hard sustainable, final 400 m progressive.
validityChecks: distance must be 1609.344 m or clearly marked as approximate
record splits if available
avoid downhill or wind-assisted route for benchmark validity
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest every 4-8 weeks or at the end of a training block.

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
protocols.limitation: Short time trials are sensitive to pacing, anaerobic ability, warmup quality, and track accuracy. They are not enough alone to model longer-distance endurance.

protocols.name: 3K Time Trial
protocols.purpose: Create a high-quality endurance-speed benchmark for 3K-5K fitness, race pace calibration, and optional critical speed input.
bestUsedFor: Intermediate runners preparing for 3K, 5K, track, or road racing.
Users needing a benchmark less long than 5K but more aerobic than a mile.
Critical Speed Lab users needing one maximal trial.
notIdealFor: New runners who cannot run hard for 12-18 minutes.
Users testing too soon after a race or hard session.
Routes with substantial hills or GPS inaccuracy.
protocolSteps: Use a track, certified route, or very reliable measured loop.
Warm up with strides so race effort does not shock the system.
Run the first kilometer controlled, hold hard rhythm, then press in the final kilometer.
Record total time and kilometer/lap splits.
Flag the result if pacing collapse, route error, or weather distortion occurred.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 15-25 min easy + drills + 4 x 20 sec strides + 3-5 min easy before start.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run 3000 m as a controlled maximal effort.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Aim for even pacing; avoid a first-kilometer surge that causes severe fade.
validityChecks: finish time must be manually entered
distance must be measured
surface and conditions should be recorded for retest comparison
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest after 6-8 weeks or use as part of a multi-trial CS protocol.

outputCalculations.formula: standard deviation of split paces / average pace × 100
outputCalculations.label: pacing quality estimate

outputCalculations.formula: distance_m and finish_time_sec stored as CS trial pair
outputCalculations.label: input for CS model

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
protocols.limitation: 3K performance can overrepresent speed-oriented runners and underrepresent longer endurance. Use with other tests for broader modeling.

protocols.name: 5K Time Trial
protocols.purpose: Establish a practical endurance benchmark for 5K pace, race prediction, training pace ranges, and progress tracking.
bestUsedFor: Runners training for 5K/10K or needing a common benchmark distance.
Users who want a field result that maps well to race and training calculators.
Critical Speed users who need a longer maximal trial.
notIdealFor: New runners not yet ready for sustained hard running.
Users with injury pain or poor recovery.
Testing on a route with traffic stops, major hills, or GPS instability.
protocolSteps: Use a measured 5K route, track equivalent, or race result.
Warm up enough to start near target rhythm.
Run the first kilometer controlled, kilometers 2-4 hard and steady, final kilometer strong.
Record finish time and splits.
Mark the test invalid or low-confidence if stopped, walked unintentionally, or route was inaccurate.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 15-25 min easy + mobility + 4 x 15-20 sec strides + short reset.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run 5K as a controlled maximal or race-equivalent effort.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Prefer even or slight negative split; avoid sprinting the first kilometer.
validityChecks: manual finish time required
distance must be 5.00 km or route must be labelled approximate
GPS moving-time vs elapsed-time choice must be explicit
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest every 6-10 weeks or use actual 5K race result.

outputCalculations.formula: derive zones using selected transparent factor model from manual result
outputCalculations.label: model estimate

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
protocols.limitation: A solo 5K time trial may differ from race performance due to motivation, course, weather, pacing, and measurement error.

protocols.name: 20-Minute Threshold Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate threshold pace from a hard, even 20-minute effort and use it as a manual anchor for threshold-based pace zones.
bestUsedFor: Intermediate and advanced runners who can pace hard but controlled for 20 minutes.
Users needing threshold pace for tempo, cruise interval, and zone calculators.
Runners who prefer pace-based testing over HR-only testing.
notIdealFor: New runners without steady pacing skill.
Users who turn threshold testing into an all-out sprint-and-fade effort.
Extreme heat/humidity or hilly routes.
protocolSteps: Use a flat route, track, or treadmill with stable calibration.
After warmup, start a 20-minute continuous hard effort.
Effort should be very hard but controlled, not a sprint from the start.
Record distance covered and optional HR data.
Use threshold estimate only if pacing remained stable without major fade.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 15-25 min easy + drills + 4 strides + 3-5 min easy reset.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run 20 minutes hard and even; calculate average pace from distance covered.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Controlled discomfort; effort should feel sustainable for the test duration without a major fade.
validityChecks: duration fixed at 1200 seconds
distance must be measured
large positive split lowers confidence
HR can lag; pace is the primary output
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest every 6-8 weeks or after a threshold-focused block.

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
protocols.limitation: A 20-minute test estimates threshold pace but does not directly measure lactate threshold or MLSS. Result depends heavily on pacing and conditions.

protocols.name: 30-Minute LTHR Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate lactate-threshold heart rate from the average HR in the final 20 minutes of a 30-minute solo hard effort.
bestUsedFor: Runners using HR-based training zones.
Intermediate/advanced users with reliable chest strap or accurate optical HR.
Users who can perform a controlled 30-minute hard effort.
notIdealFor: Users without reliable HR data.
New runners, heat-stressed runners, or anyone with cardiovascular symptoms.
Users expecting laboratory lactate threshold measurement.
protocolSteps: Use a chest strap if available and ensure HR recording is active.
Warm up thoroughly.
Run 30 minutes as a hard solo effort with steady pacing.
Do not press lap for output until the test starts; then use the final 20 min HR average as LTHR estimate.
Record optional distance and pace for additional threshold pace comparison.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 15-25 min easy + drills + 4 strides + short reset.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Run 30 minutes hard and steady; use average HR of the final 20 minutes as estimated LTHR.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: First 10 minutes controlled-hard, final 20 minutes strong and even.
validityChecks: HR sensor must be reliable
avoid HR spikes/dropouts
conditions should be moderate
do not use if the effort was not steady
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest after 6-10 weeks, ideally under similar temperature and fatigue conditions.

outputCalculations.formula: zone bpm = estimated_lthr_bpm × selected percentage range
outputCalculations.label: model estimate

outputCalculations.formula: compare HR and pace/speed first vs second segment
outputCalculations.label: quality check

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
Heart-rate testing is not cardiovascular screening.
Age-estimated HRmax should not be treated as an individual maximum.
protocols.limitation: LTHR field estimate depends on HR sensor quality, heat, fatigue, caffeine, hydration, and pacing. It is not a blood lactate test.

protocols.name: 2-Point Critical Speed Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate Critical Speed and D′ from two maximal trials of different durations or distances.
bestUsedFor: Advanced users who understand maximal testing and need CS/D′ calculations.
Runners with reliable measured distances and recovery between trials.
Users building transparent CS-based pace zones without AI prediction.
notIdealFor: Beginners, injured users, or users unable to perform two valid maximal efforts.
Trials that are too similar in duration or not maximal.
Testing both trials on the same day without adequate recovery unless intentionally designed and clearly flagged.
protocolSteps: Select two maximal trials that are meaningfully different in duration, such as a short trial and a longer trial.
Use the same or comparable surface and environmental conditions where possible.
Run each trial maximally and record exact distance and time.
Enter both distance-time pairs into Track.Lab.
Review CS, critical pace, and D′ with limitation warnings.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: For each trial: 15-25 min easy + drills + 3-4 strides + reset. Full recovery between separate trials is preferred.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Use two valid maximal distance-time performances to calculate CS and D′.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Each trial should be best sustainable effort for that distance, not a workout repetition.
validityChecks: both trials must be maximal
times must differ enough to avoid unstable slope
distance measurement must be accurate
large environmental differences lower confidence
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Update CS only when both trials are refreshed or when a new valid race result replaces one trial.

outputCalculations.formula: D′ / (target_speed - CS), valid only when target_speed > CS
outputCalculations.label: model estimate

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
CS/D′ is a performance model, not a physiological diagnosis.
Do not calculate TTE above CS when target speed is equal to or below CS.
protocols.limitation: Two-point CS can be unstable if trials are not maximal, too close in duration, or affected by different conditions. No single CS testing method is universally optimal.

protocols.name: 3-Point Critical Speed Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate Critical Speed and D′ using three maximal trials and a linear distance-time regression for better robustness than a simple 2-point model.
bestUsedFor: Advanced runners or analysts who can collect three valid maximal trials.
Users wanting a more robust CS estimate with residual/outlier visibility.
Track.Lab advanced mode users.
notIdealFor: Beginners or users who cannot recover between maximal trials.
Users with inconsistent surfaces, weather, or inaccurate timing.
Users wanting a simple beginner-friendly benchmark.
protocolSteps: Choose three maximal trials with sufficiently different durations.
Collect each trial on a measured route or track under comparable conditions.
Record exact distance and elapsed time.
Enter all three distance-time pairs.
Track.Lab fits distance = CS × time + D′ and reports CS, D′, and residual quality.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: For each trial: 15-25 min easy + dynamic drills + 3-4 strides + full reset. Separate days or full recovery are preferred.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Use three valid maximal trials to fit a distance-time regression model.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Each trial should be maximal for the selected distance, with no intentional workout pacing.
validityChecks: three valid distance-time pairs required
trials should span different durations
flag outlier residuals
do not hide poor model fit
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest when fitness changes substantially or when one trial is clearly outdated.

outputCalculations.formula: slope of linear regression: distance_m = CS × time_sec + D′
outputCalculations.label: model-derived

safetyNotes: Do not perform maximal or near-maximal tests when sick, injured, unusually fatigued, dizzy, or experiencing chest pain.
Use a safe, measured route or track; avoid traffic, extreme heat, storms, smoke, slippery surfaces, and poor visibility.
Warm up before hard tests and stop immediately if symptoms feel abnormal.
Track.Lab must label all results as field estimates, not medical diagnosis or lab testing.
Use model-fit warnings; do not present CS as exact physiology.
High residuals mean the protocol should be reviewed, not hidden.
protocols.limitation: Three-point CS is more robust than two-point but still depends on maximal effort quality, trial spacing, route accuracy, and model assumptions.

protocols.name: HR Recovery Test
protocols.purpose: Quantify how many beats per minute HR drops after a standardized hard effort, using 1-minute and optional 2-minute recovery values as non-medical recovery metrics.
bestUsedFor: Users who want a simple repeatable recovery-response metric.
Runners with reliable HR data and a repeatable test setup.
Recovery Check Lab users tracking trends over time.
notIdealFor: Users seeking medical screening or diagnosis.
Users with chest pain, faintness, palpitations, or other concerning symptoms.
Users without reliable HR measurement.

requiredInputs.label: Peak HR immediately at test end

protocolSteps: Use the same pre-test effort each time, such as a controlled hard 3-5 min segment or after a time trial.
At the end of the effort, record peak HR.
Recover using a standardized mode: standing, walking, or easy jogging.
Record HR exactly after 1 minute and optionally after 2 minutes.
Compare only against the user's own previous tests with the same recovery mode.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 10-20 min easy + optional short progressive segment before the standardized hard effort.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Measure HR drop from peak to 1 minute and optionally 2 minutes post-effort.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Use consistent pre-test intensity and consistent recovery mode.
validityChecks: HR sensor should be reliable
recovery posture/mode must be recorded
do not compare standing HRR to jogging HRR directly
not a readiness score or medical diagnostic result
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Use as a trend metric after similar workouts or standardized test sessions.

safetyNotes: This is not medical testing, not cardiovascular diagnosis, and not a readiness score.
Stop and seek appropriate care if chest symptoms, faintness, abnormal shortness of breath, or concerning symptoms occur.
HRR comparison must use the same recovery mode and similar conditions.
protocols.limitation: HR recovery is affected by intensity, heat, hydration, caffeine, stress, sleep, sensor error, posture, and recovery mode. Use only for personal trend tracking.

protocols.name: Sweat Rate Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate individual sweat rate from body mass change, fluid intake, urine output, and duration, then support hydration planning.
bestUsedFor: Long-run, half-marathon, marathon, trail, and hot-weather users.
Users wanting personalized fluid planning rather than generic fluid targets.
Fuel & Hydration Lab users.
notIdealFor: Users expecting clinical hydration analysis.
Runs where clothing is soaked and retained water distorts body mass.
Users without an accurate scale.
protocolSteps: Weigh before the run with minimal clothing and dry skin.
Record all fluid consumed during the run.
Record urine output if it occurs during the test.
Run at the target condition/intensity you want to plan for.
After the run, towel dry and weigh again as soon as practical.
Enter data and label environmental condition.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: No hard warmup required; use the planned workout warmup if paired with another run.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Estimate fluid loss from body mass change adjusted for fluid intake and urine output.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Use a representative pace/intensity for the condition you want to plan.
validityChecks: scale should be consistent
pre/post clothing should be similar and dry/toweled
duration must be in hours
record temperature/humidity for comparison
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Repeat in cool, moderate, and hot conditions if user wants condition-specific estimates.

safetyNotes: Do not overdrink to chase 100% replacement without considering comfort and hyponatremia risk.
Sweat rate is condition-specific and changes with heat, humidity, intensity, acclimation, and clothing.
Not a medical hydration test.
protocols.limitation: Field sweat rate is affected by scale precision, clothing water retention, unrecorded fluid/urine, gut contents, and environmental changes.

protocols.name: Treadmill Calibration Test
protocols.purpose: Compare treadmill-reported speed/pace against external measurement or known belt behavior, then label treadmill sessions with calibration confidence.
bestUsedFor: Users frequently training on treadmill.
Users comparing treadmill pace to outdoor pace.
Treadmill Lab users calculating speed, incline, ACSM VO2, or grade cost.
notIdealFor: Users without permission/safety to mark or measure treadmill belt.
High-speed testing without safety clip or clear space.
Users treating calibration as exact lab certification.
protocolSteps: Use treadmill safety clip and stable footwear.
Warm up at easy speed.
Run or walk at a fixed displayed speed for a fixed duration.
Capture external distance if available via calibrated footpod or safe belt measurement method.
Calculate displayed vs measured speed difference.
Store calibration note by treadmill model/speed range.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 5-10 min easy treadmill walk/jog before measurement.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Compare treadmill displayed speed to measured or external speed estimate.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Hold speed constant; do not change incline or speed mid-test.
validityChecks: speed must be constant
external measurement method must be recorded
different speeds may have different errors
do not perform unsafe belt marking
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Retest after treadmill maintenance or when changing treadmill.

safetyNotes: Use safety clip and avoid unsafe belt measurement at high speed.
Calibration result is equipment-specific and speed-specific.
Do not overcorrect workout intensity solely from one imperfect test.
protocols.limitation: Consumer treadmills, footpods, and watches all have error. Calibration should be displayed as a confidence note, not a certified correction.

protocols.name: Cadence Test
protocols.purpose: Calculate steps per minute from counted steps or device step count over a controlled segment.
bestUsedFor: Users analyzing running mechanics with simple manual data.
Runners comparing cadence across easy, tempo, and race pace.
Biomechanics Lab users needing cadence input for stride length.
notIdealFor: Users expecting injury diagnosis or form coaching.
Segments with hills, turns, traffic stops, or major pace changes.
Using inaccurate step data without validation.
protocolSteps: Choose a flat straight segment.
Reach the pace you want to measure.
Count every foot contact for 30-60 seconds or use device step count for the selected segment.
Record duration and step count.
Repeat at different paces if comparing cadence by intensity.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: 5-10 min easy running before measurement if not already warmed up.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Count total steps over a known duration.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Keep pace and effort stable during the measured segment.
validityChecks: duration should be long enough to reduce counting error
cadence means steps per minute, not strides per minute
repeat segments are better than one sample
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Measure occasionally across easy, steady, and faster paces; no need for frequent maximal testing.

safetyNotes: Do not use cadence alone as injury prediction.
Avoid forcing a target cadence aggressively; Track.Lab should only calculate and display.
Surface, pace, fatigue, and body size affect cadence.
protocols.limitation: Cadence is simple arithmetic but depends on accurate step count and stable pace. It should not be used as a standalone form diagnosis.

protocols.name: Stride Length Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate average step length or stride length from speed and cadence, or from measured distance and steps.
bestUsedFor: Users analyzing speed-cadence-stride relationships.
Runners comparing mechanics across easy, steady, and fast paces.
Biomechanics Lab users needing transparent formula output.
notIdealFor: Users seeking gait diagnosis.
Uneven terrain or segments with acceleration/deceleration.
Using stride length without clarifying step vs full gait cycle definition.
protocolSteps: Choose a flat measured segment.
Run at stable pace through the segment.
Count total steps or capture device step count.
Record distance and optional time.
Calculate average step length and, if desired, full stride cycle length.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Measure distance and count steps over a stable segment.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Avoid accelerating, braking, or weaving during the segment.
validityChecks: distance must be measured accurately
steps must count every foot contact
clearly label step length vs stride-cycle length
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Compare under similar surface and pace conditions.

safetyNotes: Do not diagnose overstriding or injury risk from this number alone.
Clarify step length vs stride length terminology in UI.
Repeat measurements are more useful than one isolated value.
protocols.limitation: The math is exact from input, but the interpretation is limited by measurement accuracy, pace, terrain, and definition choice.

protocols.name: Easy Run Drift Test
protocols.purpose: Estimate aerobic decoupling by comparing efficiency in the first and second halves of a steady easy run.
bestUsedFor: Runners monitoring aerobic durability from steady low-intensity runs.
Users with HR data and a repeatable easy route.
Load/Recovery users who want non-diagnostic drift tracking.
notIdealFor: Hot/humid days, hilly routes, trail terrain, or stop-start runs.
Users without reliable HR or pace/speed data.
Using drift as an injury or illness diagnosis.
protocolSteps: Choose a flat, repeatable easy route of sufficient duration.
Run at stable easy effort without intentional progression.
Split the run into first half and second half.
Record average speed and average HR for both halves.
Calculate efficiency factor and decoupling percentage.
Add environment notes to avoid overinterpreting heat-related drift.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: Normal easy-run warmup: first 5-10 min very easy; do not include stop-start warmup if it distorts segment averages.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Compare speed-to-HR efficiency from first half to second half of a steady easy run.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Keep route, effort, and terrain as steady as possible.
validityChecks: avoid major hills/stops
HR sensor must be reliable
heat and dehydration can distort drift
do not use as medical diagnosis
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Track trends across similar easy runs rather than reacting to one run.

safetyNotes: Decoupling is not a diagnosis and not a readiness score.
Heat, dehydration, hills, wind, cardiac drift, and sensor error can change the result.
If symptoms are abnormal, stop and seek appropriate care.
protocols.limitation: Useful as a repeatable trend metric only. Interpretation is weak when route, weather, terrain, or HR sensor quality changes.

protocols.name: Long Run Fueling Test
protocols.purpose: Test carbohydrate, fluid, and sodium intake strategy during a representative long run using manual inputs and outcome notes.
bestUsedFor: Half-marathon, marathon, ultra, trail, and long-run users.
Users practicing race fueling before event day.
Fuel & Hydration Lab users comparing gut tolerance and performance notes.
notIdealFor: Short runs where fueling is unnecessary.
Users with medical nutrition restrictions unless guided by a qualified professional.
Testing new aggressive fueling on race day.
protocolSteps: Choose a long run that resembles the target event or training condition.
Plan carb, fluid, and sodium sources before starting.
Record intake timing and totals during the run.
Record GI comfort, energy, perceived effort, and any issues.
Optionally combine with sweat-rate test using body mass change.
Review carb/hour, fluid/hour, sodium/hour, and notes; do not auto-recommend changes.
protocols.warmupSuggestion: Use the long-run warmup already planned; first 10-15 min easy is sufficient.

testExecution.primaryInstruction: Practice a selected fueling/hydration plan and calculate actual intake rates.
testExecution.pacingInstruction: Run at representative long-run or race-specific effort; do not make the workout harder just to test fuel.
validityChecks: totals must be manually entered
label product/source if user wants repeatability
do not generate medical nutrition advice
new high-carb strategies should be practiced progressively
testExecution.retestSuggestion: Repeat across different durations and weather conditions before race day.

outputCalculations.formula: total intake divided by user-selected segment interval
outputCalculations.label: planning structure

safetyNotes: This is not nutrition therapy or medical advice.
GI tolerance varies; do not force intake that causes distress.
Avoid overdrinking. Record conditions because heat changes fluid needs.
Race-day plan should be practiced before race day.
protocols.limitation: Fueling results are highly individual and condition-specific. Track.Lab should calculate observed intake and notes, not prescribe nutrition automatically.
